sections.heading: WaveGuard: Understanding and Mitigating Audio Adversarial Examples
This paper is included in the Proceedings of the 30th USENIX Security Symposium.
WaveGuard: Understanding and Mitigating Audio Adversarial Examples

figures.label: Figure 1
figures.caption: Figure 1: Depiction of an undefended ASR system and an ASR system defended by WaveGuard in the presence of a malicious adversary. The ASR system defended by WaveGuard detects the adversarial input and alerts the user.

sections.heading: 2.1 Adversarial Attacks in the Audio Domain:

figures.label: Figure 2
figures.caption: Figure 2: Top: In the targeted attack setting, the adversary solves a data-dependent optimization problem to find an additive perturbation, such that a victim ASR model transcribes the adversarial input audio to a target phrase as desired by the adversary. Bottom: In the untargeted universal attack

figures.caption: Figure 3: WaveGuard Defense Framework: We first processes the input audio $x$ using an audio transformation function $g$ to obtain $g ( x )$ . figures.label: Figure 6
figures.caption: Figure 6: Detection AUC Scores against Carlini attack at varying compression levels for the following transforms: (a) Quantization - Dequantization; (b) Downsampling - Upsampling; (c) Linear Predictive Coding (LPC); and (d) Mel Spectrogram Extraction- Inversion.

tables.label: Table 2
tables.caption: Table 2: Evaluations for each input transformation defense against various non-adaptive attacks. We use two objective metrics: AUC score and Attack Detection Accuracy for evaluation (higher values are better for both metrics).

sections.heading: 6.2 Analysis of undefended and defended transcriptions

figures.label: Figure 7
figures.caption: Figure 7: Mean Character Error Rate (CER) between the ASR transcriptions of un-transformed $( x )$ and transformed $( g ( x ) )$ audio. $C E R ( o r i g , g ( o r i g ) )$ and $C E R ( a d \nu , g ( a d \nu ) )$ indicate the CER between transcriptions of $x$ and $g ( x )$ for benign and adversarial

tables.label: table-1
tables.caption: timing comparisons for all implementations on the Intel Xeon CPU platform. The average inference time over the test set for Mozilla Deepspeech ASR model is 2.540 seconds and that of Google Lingvo ASR model is 4.212 seconds on the Intel Xeon CPU Platform. tables.caption: Figure 8: Sample transcriptions of un-transformed $( x )$ and transformed audio $( g ( x ) )$ for both benign and adversarial examples.
